items.mistake: Treating deep learning as synonymous with AI or machine learning
items.fix: Place DL correctly: AI ⊃ ML ⊃ DL. Classical ML and symbolic methods are still AI; DL is one ML approach among several.
items.why: Misclassification leads to wrong tool choices and unrealistic expectations about what DL alone can solve.
items.example: A 500-row spreadsheet regression does not require a neural network — logistic regression or a tree may be sufficient.

items.mistake: Assuming any neural network is deep learning
items.fix: Reserve DL for multi-layer networks aimed at representation learning. A single linear layer or tiny net without learned hierarchy is often shallow ML, not the DL paradigm in practice.
items.why: The roadmap uses DL to mean depth plus learned features, not merely any graph of neurons.
items.example: One hidden layer on four numeric features can be ML with a neural parameterization; ResNet-scale vision stacks are deep learning.

items.mistake: Skipping data and compute planning before choosing DL
items.fix: Estimate dataset size, label quality, latency budget, and hardware. Start with a simpler baseline; adopt DL when representation learning and scale justify cost.
items.why: Deep models underfit with too little data and waste resources when classical features already capture the signal.
items.example: Team trains a 50M-parameter vision model on 200 images — severe overfitting while a linear model on HOG features generalizes.
